subject: ap-chemistry
title: Thermochemistry & Calorimetry
stimulus:
$4\text{Fe}(s) + 3\text{O}_2(g) \rightarrow 2\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3(s)$, $\Delta H^\circ_{rxn} = -1650 \text{ kJ/mol}$

A student investigates a reaction used in hand warmers, represented above. The student mixes Fe(s) with a catalyst and sand in a small open container. The student measures the temperature of the mixture as the reaction proceeds. The data are given in the following table.

| Time (min) | Temperature of Mixture (°C) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 22.0 |
| 1 | 25.1 |
| 2 | 34.6 |
| 3 | 37.3 |
| 4 | 39.7 |
| 5 | 39.4 |

parts.prompt: The mixture (Fe(s), catalyst, and sand) has a total mass of 15.0 g and a specific heat capacity of 0.72 J/(g·°C). Calculate the amount of heat absorbed by the mixture from 0 minutes to 4 minutes.
parts.scoring_notes: Use $q = mc\Delta T$. Mass = 15.0 g, $c = 0.72$ J/(g·°C), $\Delta T = 39.7 - 22.0 = 17.7$°C. $q = (15.0)(0.72)(17.7) = 191$ J (accept 190–192 J depending on sig figs).

scoring_points.description: For the correct calculated value with appropriate units: 1 point

alternatives.correct_example:
$q = mc\Delta T = (15.0\text{ g})(0.72\text{ J/g·°C})(17.7\text{ °C}) \approx 191\text{ J}$

Use $\Delta T = T_{final} - T_{initial} = 39.7 - 22.0 = 17.7$°C (time 4 min is the maximum temperature). Units are joules (J).

wrong_examples: Using $\Delta T = 39.4 - 22.0 = 17.4$°C (time 5 min) — use the maximum temperature at 4 min, not 5 min.
Omitting units — the answer must include J or kJ.
common_traps: $q = mc\Delta T$. Use the peak temperature (4 min = 39.7°C) for $T_{final}$, not time 5 min. $\Delta T$ must be positive since heat is absorbed by the mixture.
scoring_points.official_rubric: For the correct calculated value with units: 1 point. $q = mc\Delta T = (15.0\text{ g})(0.72\text{ J/(g·°C)})(39.7 - 22.0\text{ °C}) = 190\text{ J}$.

sample_responses.commentary: Correct application of $q = mc\Delta T$ using peak temperature at 4 min.
Used temperature at 5 min rather than the maximum temperature at 4 min.

parts.prompt: Calculate the mass of Fe(s) that reacted to generate the amount of heat calculated in part (a).
parts.scoring_notes: Step 1: Convert $q_{surr}$ to $q_{rxn}$: $q_{rxn} = -q_{surr} = -191$ J = $-0.191$ kJ. Moles of reaction = $\frac{-0.191\text{ kJ}}{-1650\text{ kJ/mol}} = 1.16 \times 10^{-4}$ mol rxn. Step 2: Convert mol rxn to mol Fe and then grams. Per balanced equation, 4 mol Fe per mol rxn. $n(\text{Fe}) = 4 \times 1.16 \times 10^{-4} = 4.64 \times 10^{-4}$ mol. Mass = $4.64 \times 10^{-4} \times 55.85 = 0.0259$ g Fe (accept 0.026–0.027 g).

scoring_points.description: For the correct calculated moles of reaction consistent with part (a): 1 point

alternatives.correct_example:
Since the mixture (surroundings) absorbs $q_{surr} = +191$ J, the reaction releases $q_{rxn} = -191$ J $= -0.191$ kJ.
$n_{rxn} = \dfrac{0.191\text{ kJ}}{1650\text{ kJ/mol}} = 1.16 \times 10^{-4}\text{ mol rxn}$

(Accept consistent answer based on student's part (a) value.)

wrong_examples: Using $\Delta H = -1650$ kJ and dividing into kJ directly without sign reconciliation — set up ratios carefully.
Not converting J to kJ before dividing by $\Delta H$ in kJ/mol — units must match.
common_traps: Sign convention: $q_{surr} > 0$ means exothermic reaction; $q_{rxn} = -q_{surr}$. Divide the magnitude of heat by the magnitude of $\Delta H^\circ_{rxn}$ to get moles of reaction. The stoichiometric coefficient of the reaction as written matters for the per-mol quantity.
scoring_points.official_rubric: For the correct calculated value of the moles of reaction, consistent with part (a) (may be implicit): 1 point. $n_{rxn} = -q_{sys}/\Delta H^\circ = 0.190\text{ kJ}/1650\text{ kJ/mol} = 1.15\times10^{-4}\text{ mol rxn}$.

sample_responses.commentary: Correct sign reconciliation; consistent with SG calculation.
Failed to convert J to kJ before dividing by $\Delta H$ in kJ/mol.

scoring_points.description: For the correct mass of Fe consistent with moles of reaction from b1: 1 point

required_elements: $m(\text{Fe}) = 1.16 \times 10^{-4}\text{ mol rxn} \times \dfrac{4\text{ mol Fe}}{1\text{ mol rxn}} \times \dfrac{55.85\text{ g}}{1\text{ mol Fe}} \approx 0.026\text{ g}$
alternatives.correct_example:
$n(\text{Fe}) = 1.16 \times 10^{-4}\text{ mol rxn} \times \dfrac{4\text{ mol Fe}}{1\text{ mol rxn}} = 4.64 \times 10^{-4}\text{ mol Fe}$
$m(\text{Fe}) = 4.64 \times 10^{-4}\text{ mol} \times 55.85\text{ g/mol} = 0.0259\text{ g}$

wrong_examples: Using 1 mol Fe per mol rxn instead of 4 — the balanced equation shows $4\text{Fe}$ per formula unit.
Using molar mass of $\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$ instead of Fe — the question asks for mass of Fe.
common_traps: From balanced equation: $4\text{Fe} + 3\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{Fe}_2\text{O}_3$. Ratio is 4 mol Fe per mole of reaction as written. Molar mass of Fe = 55.85 g/mol. Follow through from b1.
scoring_points.official_rubric: For the correct calculated value of the mass of iron: 1 point. $1.16\times10^{-4}\text{ mol rxn} \times (4\text{ mol Fe/mol rxn}) \times (55.85\text{ g/mol}) = 0.027\text{ g Fe}$ (0.026 g if decimals carried).

sample_responses.commentary: Correct stoichiometric factor (4 mol Fe per mol rxn) and molar mass applied.
Used 1 mol Fe per mol rxn; the balanced equation shows 4 Fe per formula unit.

parts.prompt: In a second experiment, the student uses twice the mass of iron as that calculated in part (b) but the same mass of sand as in the first experiment. Would the maximum temperature reached in the second experiment be greater than, less than, or equal to the maximum temperature in the first experiment? Justify your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: Greater than. With twice the mass of Fe, more moles of Fe can react, generating more heat ($q_{rxn}$ larger in magnitude). The heat capacity of the mixture is approximately the same (sand mass unchanged, Fe mass small). More heat into essentially the same heat capacity → higher $\Delta T$ → higher maximum temperature.

scoring_points.description: For the correct answer ('greater than') and a valid chemical justification: 1 point

required_elements: More moles of Fe react, releasing more heat into approximately the same heat capacity, resulting in a larger temperature increase.
alternatives.correct_example: Greater than. Doubling the mass of Fe provides more moles of reactant, so more moles of reaction occur, releasing more total heat. The heat capacity of the mixture (dominated by sand) is approximately unchanged, so the same mass absorbs more heat, leading to a greater temperature rise.

wrong_examples: Saying 'equal to' — more Fe means more reaction, more heat, so the temperature rise must be greater.
Saying 'less than' — no basis; more heat released raises temperature higher.
common_traps: $q_{rxn} \propto$ moles of limiting reagent. If Fe is doubled and O₂ (from air, open container) is not limiting, more Fe reacts, more heat is released. $\Delta T = q / (mc)$; if q increases and $mc$ is roughly constant, $\Delta T$ increases.
scoring_points.official_rubric: For the correct answer and a valid justification: 1 point. Greater than. A greater mass of iron provides a greater number of moles of reaction, transferring a greater quantity of thermal energy to the same mass of sand, leading to a greater maximum temperature.

sample_responses.response: Greater than. More Fe reacts, releasing more heat. The same mass of sand (same heat capacity) absorbs more heat, so $\Delta T$ is larger and the maximum temperature is higher.
sample_responses.commentary: Correct answer with valid reasoning linking more Fe to more heat to higher temperature.

sample_responses.response: Equal to. The same amount of sand is used so the temperature rise is the same.
sample_responses.commentary: More Fe means more heat released; even with the same sand mass, $\Delta T$ increases with more heat input.
